bundles.changelog: First concrete foundation-bundle prerelease package (six foundation-document templates with their own schema and hash baselines).
Reworks the technical-architecture document so cross-cutting content lives in one place and is cross-referenced rather than duplicated.
Your built system can now APPLY foundation-document upgrades (not just detect them); upgrades became repeatable, quieter, and clearer, and merge your edits with the new version where possible.

bundles.recommendation_reason: You now build and run your system one capability at a time, accepting each before the next.
bundles.changelog: You now build and run your system one capability at a time, accepting each before building the next (the build-and-operate loop with per-phase acceptance).

bundles.recommendation_reason: Adds operator orientation and risk-action protections, and lets your system safely apply its own updates.
bundles.changelog: Your system now keeps you oriented and protects risky actions; can receive operating-layer improvements through an upgrade (not just foundation documents); and can check for and safely apply its own updates.

bundles.recommendation_reason: The approval drill now uses an example grounded in what your system actually does.
bundles.changelog: The approval drill (the one-time safety demonstration) now uses an example grounded in what your system actually does, instead of a generic action.

bundles.recommendation_reason: Makes updating your system one safe step and hardens two update-safety details.
bundles.changelog: Updating your system is now one safe step: the assistant refreshes the update tool and applies the update together. Also hardens two safety details — the record of an approved update can no longer be altered, and the start-of-session update check uses the same trusted source as the manual check.

bundles.recommendation_reason: Fixes a safety-check bug that was interrupting your ordinary edits and training you to click through every prompt.
bundles.changelog: Fixes a safety-check bug that was stopping you to approve ordinary edits to your own notes and documents — everyday words like "firm" or "High" were mistaken for risky commands, which both broke auto mode and trained you to click through every prompt. The check now decides by what an action actually does, so it pauses only for genuinely risky, irreversible, or outgoing actions. Also adds a short note explaining why auto mode still pauses before sending, sharing, or deleting.

bundles.recommendation_reason: Stops your system from talking you out of safe updates — the update check now reports a clear recommendation and never treats a normal prerelease label or missing notes as a reason to wait.
bundles.changelog: Fixes the update flow so your system no longer talks you out of safe updates. When you ask "what's new?", it now runs the update check and reports exactly what the check found — a plain-language summary, the system's own recommendation, and the one command to apply it — instead of a vague walkthrough. A version being labeled prerelease or missing detailed notes is never treated as a reason to wait, and if your update tool is behind, the check routes you straight to the one-step refresh-and-apply.

bundles.recommendation_reason: Makes updates safer to approve: your system now shows you a read-only preview of exactly what an update changes before anything is applied, and the apply installs exactly what you previewed.
bundles.changelog: Improves how updates are previewed and applied. When an update is available, your system now runs a read-only preview that shows exactly what would change -- and that preview works even when the update tool is behind, so it can never wrongly tell you there is 'nothing to show'. You then approve, and the apply installs exactly the version you previewed (if the official source moved in between, it stops and re-previews rather than installing something different). The apply command is also kept short so it can't break when pasted.

bundles.recommendation_reason: Makes updating smoother: your system keeps offering the remind-later / skip / not-now choices for an update, and once you approve it runs the update for you instead of asking you to type a command.
bundles.changelog: Smooths out the update experience. When an update is available your system keeps offering the full set of choices -- see what's new, remind me later, skip this version, or not now -- even if you've asked it to lead with a single next step; remind-later and skip are remembered so the reminder actually pauses. And once you approve an update, the assistant runs the update commands for you (resolving the tool's location so they can't fail with 'command not found') rather than asking you to paste them into a terminal.

bundles.recommendation_reason: A small consistency fix to the update process -- your system now follows one clear path to check for and apply updates.
bundles.changelog: Simplifies and makes consistent how updates are checked and applied. Your system now uses one clear path -- it checks (which reports what's new and its recommendation), and once you approve, the assistant applies the update for you. A separate preview step that the system's own instructions did not actually use has been removed, so the update flow behaves the same way every time.

bundles.recommendation_reason: A small reliability fix to the update process -- update commands now always use the tool's full path, so checking for and applying updates never fails with a 'command not found' message.
bundles.changelog: Makes the update process more reliable. When the system checks for or applies an update, it now always runs the update tool by its full path instead of a short name that was sometimes not recognized -- so the update step no longer trips over a 'command not found' message. Nothing about your data or what updates do has changed.

bundles.recommendation_reason: Your system now knows where to put its outputs and how to format them for the right channel — outputs land in a predictable place, and significant messages get a design pass before they go out.
bundles.changelog: Your system now produces outputs in a consistent, predictable location (a deliverables/ folder with a clear naming convention established at setup). It also ships maintained output-quality guidance — channel-appropriate formatting for email and SMS, clearer message organization — that your agents consult when producing anything you will read or send. A design-pass skill runs before significant outbound messages to check rendering and organization against your preferences before anything is sent.

bundles.recommendation_reason: When your system writes back to an outside destination, it now does so through a checked path — values are validated against your allowed set, significant writes get a fresh plain-language approval, and a build-time check flags anything that tries to go around the safe path.
bundles.changelog: Adds write-integrity protection for systems that send data back out to an external destination (a spreadsheet, a tracker, an outside service). Writes now go through a checked path: each value is validated against the set of values you allow before it leaves, significant writes are proposed to you in plain language and recorded with your approval, and a build-time check flags any write that tries to bypass the safe path. Each agent's permitted write destinations are derived and shown in a single audit table. Systems that do not write back to anything carry none of this — it is added only where it is needed. Your own data, rules, credentials, and logs are untouched.

bundles.recommendation_reason: Adds an integrity contract for changes your system makes to data outside itself — independent verification, copy-first proof, and proven undo.
bundles.changelog: Changes your system makes to data outside itself now carry an integrity contract: success is confirmed through an independent route (never by re-reading its own write), new write logic is proven on a copy before first live use, and undo is proven to restore your data.

bundles.recommendation_reason: Your system can now safely take on new capabilities you ask for mid-run — it defines, builds, and gets your acceptance before anything goes live.
bundles.changelog: Adds the operator-originated-enhancement flow: when you ask for something outside the current plan, your system now defines it, builds it, and records your acceptance before it goes live — with a build-time coverage check and a runtime write gate that ensure every capability that writes outside your system is declared, risk-classified, and approved by you. Includes a new add-capability skill, a machine-readable record of your system's write-back capabilities and their acceptance state, and an always-on commit-hygiene guard that keeps a clean baseline before building and commits each accepted phase as its own revertable unit. Systems that never write outside themselves carry none of the write-gating. Your own data, rules, credentials, and logs are untouched.

bundles.recommendation_reason: Your system can now test a new capability that changes things outside itself before you accept it — on a small, capped slice of your real data with a proven undo path, or in a preview that changes nothing at all.
bundles.changelog: When your system is trying out a new capability that changes things outside itself, it can now safely test it two new ways before you accept it — a small, capped batch on your real data (with a proven undo path), or a preview run that changes nothing at all. A capped limit and your per-item approval bound the small-batch test; the preview never writes anything.

bundles.recommendation_reason: When you come back to your system, it now leads with the step you actually left off on instead of an unrelated item off a date list, and it never records a guess about a future version check.
bundles.changelog: When you come back to your system — at the start of a session or after a pause — it now leads with the step you actually left off on (including a paused or half-finished task), instead of picking an unrelated item off a date list. And when it saves where things stand, it records only the version you are on right now, never a guess like "no update expected next time" that a later check could prove wrong.

bundles.recommendation_reason: Future writes-back capabilities get more proven building blocks, and if something you already had running no longer follows your system's current safety rules, an update now catches it and pauses it safely instead of breaking it.
bundles.changelog: Two additions under the hood. An adapter registry enforces a cap on how many things a single write action can touch; a read-only view lets a capability be built and tested without the code that builds it ever being able to touch the credential that can write; and a clearer three-way split between the code your system trusts by default, the code a connected service's adapter runs, and the code a specific capability runs makes it easier to see what any given piece of code is and is not allowed to do. A Gmail adapter (label and filter actions only — never sending, never permanently deleting) proves these building blocks out as a second real capability. And when your system checks for and applies an update, it now also checks whether anything you already had running still follows the current safety rules — if something no longer does, that one thing is safely paused, reversibly, and handed to the same define → build → accept process you already use to add a new capability, rather than left running unprotected or broken outright. No breaking changes: everything your system could already do continues to work exactly as before, and every previously released version still installs and runs correctly.
